subject: Physics
q: One main scale division of a vernier caliper is equal to $m$ units. If $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ division of main scale coincides with $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale, the least count of the vernier caliper is :
question: One main scale division of a vernier caliper is equal to $m$ units. If $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ division of main scale coincides with $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale, the least count of the vernier caliper is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n} M S D=(\mathrm{n}+1) \mathrm{VSD} \\ & \Rightarrow 1 \mathrm{VSD}=\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}+1} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{C}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\ & \mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{m}-\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}+1}\right) \\ & \mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{C}=\mathrm{m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{n}+1-\mathrm{n}}{\mathrm{n}+1}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{C}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}+1}\right)\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
question: The diameter of a sphere is measured using a vernier caliper whose 9 divisions of main scale are equal to 10 divisions of vernier scale. The shortest division on the main scale is equal to $1 \mathrm{~mm}$. The main scale reading is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and second division of vernier scale coincides with a division on main scale. If mass of the sphere is 8.635 g, the density of the sphere is:
solution: Given $9 \mathrm{MSD}=10 \mathrm{VSD}$ $\operatorname{mass}=8.635 \mathrm{~g}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-1 \mathrm{VSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{MSD}-\frac{9}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{MSD} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=0.01 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Reading of diameter $=\mathrm{MSR}+\mathrm{LC} \times \mathrm{VSR}$ $\begin{aligned} & =2 \mathrm{~cm}+(0.01) \times(2) \\ & =2.02 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$ Volume of sphere $=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3=\frac{4}{3} \pi\left(\frac{2.02}{2}\right)^3$ $=4.32 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ Density $=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{8.635}{4.32}=1.998 \sim 2.00 \mathrm{~g}$

When jaws are touching each other, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>7</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincides with a division of the main scale and the zero of vernier scale is lying right side of the zero of the main scale. When this vernier is used to measure the length of the cylinder the zero of the vernier scale between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> VSD coincides with the main scale division. The length of the cylinder is (VSD is vernier scale division)
question: The least count of the main scale of a vernier calipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. Its vernier scale is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions and coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of the main scale. When jaws are touching each other, the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>7</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincides with a division of the main scale and the zero of vernier scale is lying right side of the zero of the main scale. When this vernier is used to measure the length of the cylinder the zero of the vernier scale between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> VSD coincides with the main scale division. The length of the cylinder is (VSD is vernier scale division)
solution:
Zero Error = 0+7×0.1=0.070

Vernier reading = (3.1+4×0.01) –0.07=3.07

q:
The pitch and the number of divisions, on the circular scale, for a given screw gauge are 0.5 mm and 100 respectively. When the screw gauge is fully tightened without any object, the zero of its circular scale lies 3 divisions below the mean line.

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
question:

The readings of the main scale and the circular scale, for a thin sheet, are 5.5 mm and 48 respectively, the thickness of this sheet is:
solution:
least count =0.5 mm100=0.005 mm

Reading =5.5 mm+48-3×0.005 mm

=5.725 mm.

If 40 divisions are there in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions, then least count of the travelling microscope is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
question: A travelling microscope is used to determine the refractive index of a glass slab. If 40 divisions are there in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> on main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>49</mn></math> main scale divisions, then least count of the travelling microscope is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>.
solution:
Given here, main scale reading, MS=140 cm

And, 50×VS=49×MS

Least count of travelling microscope is LC=MS-VS

=MS-4950 MS

=150×MS

=150×140 cm=12000×10-2 m

=5×10-6 m

subject: Physics
q: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division $=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$, then the least count of the vernier callipers is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: In a vernier callipers, 50 vernier scale divisions are equal to 48 main scale divisions. If one main scale division $=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$, then the least count of the vernier callipers is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
Given: 50 vernier divisions = 48 main divisions and 1 main division = 0.05 mm
50 vernier divisions = 48 × 0.05 = 2.4 mm
1 vernier division = 2.4/50 = 0.048 mm
Least count = 1 main division - 1 vernier division = 0.05 - 0.048 = 0.002 mm

subject: Physics
q: Identify the physical quantity that cannot be measured using spherometer :
question: Identify the physical quantity that cannot be measured using spherometer :
options: Radius of curvature of concave surface
Radius of curvature of convex surface
solution:
A spherometer is a device used for measuring the curvature of surfaces, particularly the radius of curvature of spherical or cylindrical surfaces. It consists of a central leg with a fine screw, and three outer legs that form a tripod. The device is primarily designed for measuring dimensions related to curved surfaces.

Specific rotation is a property related to the rotation of plane-polarized light in a substance. It is not measured using a spherometer. Instead, a polarimeter is typically used to measure specific rotation.

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> are correct and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>.</mo></math>

In 5 revolution, distance travel, 5 mm.

So the least count =150=0.02

q: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: The one division of main scale of vernier callipers reads <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions on main scale. When the two jaws of the instrument touch each other the zero of the Vernier lies to the right of zero of the main scale and its fourth division coincides with a main scale division. When a spherical bob is tightly placed between the two jaws, the zero of the Vernier scale lies in between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier division coincides with a main scale division. The diameter of the bob will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Given,

1 MSD=1 mm

10 VSD=9 MSD

Then, 1 VSD=910MSD=0.9 MSD

Least count of Vernier calliper is L.C. =0.1 mm=0.01 cm

Positive zero error=0.4 mm =0.04 cm

Observed reading of diameter of spherical bob=4.1 cm+6×0.01=4.16 cm

Real reading of diameter of bob=4.16-0.04=4.12 cm =412×10-2 cm

Statement II : The vernier constant is given by one main scale division multiplied by the number of vernier scale divisions.

question:

Statement II : The vernier constant is given by one main scale division multiplied by the number of vernier scale divisions.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below.
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
solution: In general one vernier scale division is smaller than one main scale division but in some modified cases it may be not correct. Also least count is given by one main scale division / number of vernier scale division for normal vernier calliper.

subject: Physics
q: For the determination of refractive index of glass slab, a travelling microscope is used whose main scale contains 300 equal divisions equals to 15 cm. The vernier scale attached to the microscope has 25 divisions equals to 24 divisions of main scale. The least count (LC) of the travelling microscope is (in cm) :
question: For the determination of refractive index of glass slab, a travelling microscope is used whose main scale contains 300 equal divisions equals to 15 cm. The vernier scale attached to the microscope has 25 divisions equals to 24 divisions of main scale. The least count (LC) of the travelling microscope is (in cm) :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 300 \mathrm{msd}=15 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & 1 \mathrm{msd}=\frac{15}{300} \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & 25 \mathrm{vsd}=24 \mathrm{msd} \\ & 1 \mathrm{vsd}=\frac{24}{25} \mathrm{msd} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{msd}-1 \mathrm{vsd}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{LC}=1 \mathrm{msd}-\frac{24}{25} \mathrm{msd}=\frac{1}{25} \mathrm{msd} \\ & \mathrm{LC}=\frac{1}{25} \times 0.05=0.002 \mathrm{~cm}
\end{aligned}$
correct option (2)

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion(A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : In Vernier calliper if positive zero error exists, then while taking measurements, the reading taken will be more than the actual reading.
Reason (R) : The zero error in Vernier Calliper might have happened due to manufacturing defect or due to rough handling.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
If a positive zero error exists in a vernier callipers, it means that the instrument shows a reading greater than zero even when the jaws are closed and there is no object being measured. In such a case, when actual measurements are taken, the readings will be more than the actual values.

The zero error in Vernier callipers can indeed occur due to manufacturing defects or rough handling. Manufacturing defects may lead to misalignments or inaccuracies in the instrument, while rough handling can result in damage that affects the calibration of the callipers.

Hence, both (A) and (R) are correct, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge the zero of main scale reference line coincides with the fifth division of the circular scale when two studs are in contact. There are $100$ divisions in circular scale and pitch of screw gauge is $0.1$ mm. When diameter of a sphere is measured, the reading of main scale is $5$ mm and $50^{\text{th}}$ division of circular scale coincides with the reference line of main scale. The diameter of sphere is _______ mm.
question: In a screw gauge the zero of main scale reference line coincides with the fifth division of the circular scale when two studs are in contact. There are $100$ divisions in circular scale and pitch of screw gauge is $0.1$ mm. When diameter of a sphere is measured, the reading of main scale is $5$ mm and $50^{\text{th}}$ division of circular scale coincides with the reference line of main scale. The diameter of sphere is _______ mm.
solution:
Least count of the screw gauge is given by:

$\text{LC} = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{Number of divisions on circular scale}}$

$\text{LC} = \dfrac{0.1}{100} = 0.001 \text{ mm}$

When the two studs are in contact, the $5^{\text{th}}$ division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line. This indicates a positive zero error.

$\text{Zero error} = +5 \times \text{LC} = +5 \times 0.001 = +0.005 \text{ mm}$

For the measurement of the sphere's diameter:

$\text{Main scale reading (MSR)} = 5 \text{ mm}$

$\text{Circular scale reading (CSR)} = 50$

$\text{Measured diameter} = \text{MSR} + \text{CSR} \times \text{LC}$

$\text{Measured diameter} = 5 + 50 \times 0.001 = 5.050 \text{ mm}$

The true diameter is obtained by subtracting the zero error from the measured value:

$\text{True diameter} = \text{Measured diameter} - \text{Zero error}$

$\text{True diameter} = 5.050 - 0.005 = 5.045 \text{ mm}$

Answer: $5.045$

subject: Physics
q: In a metre bridge experiment the balance point is obtained if the gaps are closed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A shunt of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistor to shift the balancing point by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> is ______.
question: In a metre bridge experiment the balance point is obtained if the gaps are closed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math>. A shunt of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> resistor to shift the balancing point by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Case 1: No shunt is added

The initial balancing length l of the bridge is,

23=l100-l

⇒200-2l=3l⇒l=40 cm

Case 2: When shunt is added

The new balancing length is, l1=22.5+40=62.5 cm

So, 62.537.5=23X3+X

⇒X=2.

subject: Physics
q:
In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
[Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
question:
In a screw gauge, the zero of the circular scale lies 3 divisions above the horizontal pitch line when their metallic studs are brought in contact. Using this instrument thickness of a sheet is measured. If pitch scale reading is 1 mm and the circular scale reading is 51 then the correct thickness of the sheet is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
[Assume least count is 0.01 mm ]
solution:
For a screw gauge, the measurement formula is: Reading = Pitch scale + (Circular scale × Least count) - Zero error
The zero of circular scale lies 3 divisions above the pitch line, indicating negative zero error:
Zero error = -3 × 0.01 = -0.03 mm
Reading = 1 + (51 × 0.01) - (-0.03) = 1 + 0.51 + 0.03 = 1.54 mm

subject: Physics
q: The Vernier constant of Vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and it has zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. While measuring diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and coinciding vernier division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> . The corrected diameter will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: The Vernier constant of Vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and it has zero error of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. While measuring diameter of a sphere, the main scale reading is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> and coinciding vernier division is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> . The corrected diameter will be _____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Vernier constant is the least count of the vernier V.C=0.1 mm=0.01 cm

zero error =-0.05 cm

∴correction=0.05 cm

M.S.R.=1.7 cm

V.S.R.=5 V.C.=5×0.01=0.05 cm

The diameter of the sphere will be,

diameter=MSR+VSR+correction

1.7+0.05+0.05=1.8 cm

=180×10-2 cm

subject: Physics
q: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>20</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
question: In a Screw Gauge, fifth division of the circular scale coincides with the reference line when the ratchet is closed. There are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions on the circular scale, and the main scale moves by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> on a complete rotation. For a particular observation the reading on the main scale is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>20</mn><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> division of the circular scale coincides with reference line. Calculate the true reading.
solution:
L.C. of screw Gauge=0.550=0.01 mm
Zero error of screw Gauge =5×L.C.=5×0.01=0.05 mm
Reading of screw gauge = main scale reading + circular scale reading ×L.C.-Zero error =5+20×0.01-0.05=5.15 mm

subject: Physics
q: In the experimental set up of metre bridge shown in the figure, the null point is obtaine data distance of $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from A. If a $10 \Omega$ resistor is connected in series with $\mathrm{R}_{1},$ the null point shifts by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The resistance that should be connected in parallel with $\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+10\right) \Omega$ such that the null point shifts back to its initial position is
question: In the experimental set up of metre bridge shown in the figure, the null point is obtaine data distance of $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from A. If a $10 \Omega$ resistor is connected in series with $\mathrm{R}_{1},$ the null point shifts by $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The resistance that should be connected in parallel with $\left(\mathrm{R}_{1}+10\right) \Omega$ such that the null point shifts back to its initial position is
solution:
Initially at null deflection $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=\frac{2}{3}$$\ldots(\mathrm{i})$

Finally at null deflection, when null point is shifted $\frac{\mathrm{R}_{1}+10}{\mathrm{R}_{2}}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{1}+10=\mathrm{R}_{2}$...(ii)

Solving equations (i) and (ii) we get $\frac{2 R_{2}}{3}+10=R_{2}$

$10=\frac{\mathrm{R}_{2}}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{2}=30 \Omega$

$\& R_{1}=20 \Omega$

Now if required resistance is $\mathrm{R}$ then $\frac{\frac{30 \times \mathrm{R}}{30+\mathrm{R}}}{30}=\frac{2}{3}$

$\mathrm{R}=60 \Omega$

subject: Physics
q: A travelling microscope has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> divisions per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale while its Vernier scale has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> main scale divisions, what is the least count of the travelling microscope ?
question: A travelling microscope has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> divisions per <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale while its Vernier scale has total <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn></math> Vernier scale divisions are equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn></math> main scale divisions, what is the least count of the travelling microscope ?
solution:
One main scale division MSD=120 cm

One vernier scale division VSD=2425MSD=2425×120 cm

∴ Least count LC=MSD-VSD=1201-2425cm

=120×125=1500=0.002 cm

=0.002 cm

q: A screw gauge has 50 divisions on its circular scale. The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale marking, prior to use. Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>mm</mi></math> is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the lest count of the screw gauge, are respectively:
question: A screw gauge has 50 divisions on its circular scale. The circular scale is 4 units ahead of the pitch scale marking, prior to use. Upon one complete rotation of the circular scale, a displacement of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>mm</mi></math> is noticed on the pitch scale. The nature of zero error involved and the lest count of the screw gauge, are respectively:

subject: Physics
q: When two resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected in series and introduced into the left gap of a meter bridge and a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right gap, a null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left side. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are connected in parallel and introduced into the left gap, a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right-gap to get null point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left end. The product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>
question: When two resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> connected in series and introduced into the left gap of a meter bridge and a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right gap, a null point is found at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left side. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are connected in parallel and introduced into the left gap, a resistance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> is introduced into the right-gap to get null point at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from left end. The product of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo> </mo><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math>
solution:
A meter bridge consists of a wire of length 1 m and of uniform cross-sectional area stretched taut and clamped between two thick metallic strips bent at right angles with two gaps across which resistors are to be connected. It works on the principle of the Wheatstone bridge. Therefore, we can write

R1+R210=6040⇒R1+R2=15 Ω

Then for the second case, we can write 

R1R2R1+R2×3=4060⇒R1R2=30 Ω2

subject: Physics
q: One main scale division of a vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the main scale. The least count of the callipers in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math> is :
question: One main scale division of a vernier callipers is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the vernier scale coincide with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>th</mi></msup></math> division of the main scale. The least count of the callipers in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>mm</mi></math> is :
solution:
n-1a=na'

a'=n-1an

∴ L.C.=1MSD-1VSD

=a-a' cm

=a-n-1an

=na-na+an=an cm

=10an mm

subject: Physics
q:
When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$)
question:
When both jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, zero mark of the vernier scale is right to zero mark of main scale, $4^{\text {th }}$ mark on vernier scale coincides with certain mark on the main scale. While measuring the length of a cylinder, observer observes 15 divisions on main scale and $5^{\text {th }}$ division of vernier scale coincides with a main scale division. Measured length of cylinder is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
(Least count of Vernier calliper $=0.1 \mathrm{~mm}$)
solution:
Given Least Count ($LC$) = $0.1$ mm.
When jaws touch, the zero of the vernier scale is to the right of the main scale zero, indicating a positive zero error.
Zero error ($e$) = $+ (n \times LC)$, where $n$ is the coinciding vernier division.
$e = +(4 \times 0.1) = +0.4$ mm.
While measuring the cylinder:
Main Scale Reading ($MSR$) = $15$ divisions.
Assuming $1$ MSD = $1$ mm (standard for $LC = 0.1$ mm), $MSR = 15$ mm.
Vernier Scale Reading ($VSR$) = $5 \times LC = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5$ mm.
Observed Reading = $MSR + VSR = 15 + 0.5 = 15.5$ mm.
True Measured Length = Observed Reading $-$ Zero Error
True Length = $15.5 - 0.4 = 15.1$ mm.

subject: Physics
q: In a Vernier Caliper <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of main scale. When both jaws of Vernier calipers touch each other, the zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the left of zero of the main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> Vernier scale division exactly coincides with the main scale reading. One main scale division is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. While measuring diameter of a spherical body, the body is held between two jaws. It is now observed that zero of the Vernier scale lies between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>31</mn></math> divisions of main scale reading and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier scale division exactly. coincides with the main scale reading. The diameter of the spherical body will be:
question: In a Vernier Caliper <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> divisions of Vernier scale is equal to the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn></math> divisions of main scale. When both jaws of Vernier calipers touch each other, the zero of the Vernier scale is shifted to the left of zero of the main scale and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>4</mn><mi>th</mi></msup></math> Vernier scale division exactly coincides with the main scale reading. One main scale division is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>. While measuring diameter of a spherical body, the body is held between two jaws. It is now observed that zero of the Vernier scale lies between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>31</mn></math> divisions of main scale reading and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>6</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> Vernier scale division exactly. coincides with the main scale reading. The diameter of the spherical body will be:
solution:
Given that 1 M.S.D=1 mm

And 9 M.S.D =10 V.S.D

⇒1 V.S.D=0.9M.S.D=0.9 mm

L.C of vernier caliper =1-0.9=0.1 mm=0.01 cm

Zero error =10-4×0.1 mm=0.6 mm as at zero the, the zero of the vernier is shifted towards left zero error is negative. Therefore, zero error=-0.6 mm.

Reading =M.S.R+V.S.R-  Zero error

Reading=3+6×0.01--0.06

Reading=3+0.06+0.06

Reading=3.12 cm

The closest option is 3.10 cm

subject: Physics
q:
A screw gauge gives the following reading when used to measure the diameter of a wire.
Main scale reading : $0 \mathrm{~mm}$
Circular scale reading : 52 divisions
Given that $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions of the circular scale.
The diameter of wire from the above date is :
question:
A screw gauge gives the following reading when used to measure the diameter of a wire.
Main scale reading : $0 \mathrm{~mm}$
Circular scale reading : 52 divisions
Given that $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions of the circular scale.
The diameter of wire from the above date is :
solution: Diameter of wire $=\frac{1}{100} \times 52=0.52 \mathrm{~mm}=0.052 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: In a screw gauge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. There are<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> main scale divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible error, the thickness of the wire is
question: In a screw gauge, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> complete rotations of the screw cause it to move a linear distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. There are<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> circular scale divisions. The thickness of a wire measured by this screw gauge gives a reading of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> main scale divisions and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> circular scale divisions. Assuming negligible error, the thickness of the wire is
solution:
The Least count=0.255×100 cm=5×10-4 cm.

Reading =4×0.05 cm+30×5×10-4 cm
              =0.2+0.0150 cm=0.2150 cm

subject: Physics
q: Two full turns of the circular scale of a screw gauge cover a distance of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on its main scale. The total number of divisions on the circular scale is 50 . Further, it is found that the screw gauge has a zero error of $-0.03 \mathrm{~mm}$ while measuring the diameter of a thin wire, a student notes the main scale reading of $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the number of circular scale divisions in line with the main scale as 35 . The diameter of the wire is
question: Two full turns of the circular scale of a screw gauge cover a distance of $1 \mathrm{~mm}$ on its main scale. The total number of divisions on the circular scale is 50 . Further, it is found that the screw gauge has a zero error of $-0.03 \mathrm{~mm}$ while measuring the diameter of a thin wire, a student notes the main scale reading of $3 \mathrm{~mm}$ and the number of circular scale divisions in line with the main scale as 35 . The diameter of the wire is
solution: Diameter $=$ M.S.R. $+$ C.S.R $\times$ L.C. $+$ Z.E. $=3+35 \times(0.5 / 50)+0.03=3.38 \mathrm{~mm}$

subject: Physics
q: In a vernier callipers, each <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> equal parts. If tenth vernier scale division coincides with nineth main scale division. Then the value of vernier constant will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>
question: In a vernier callipers, each <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> on the main scale is divided into <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> equal parts. If tenth vernier scale division coincides with nineth main scale division. Then the value of vernier constant will be _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>
solution:
Vernier constant is defined as the difference between the value of one main scale division and one vernier scale division. It is also known as the least count of vernier calliper.

The main scale division of the given vernier calliper is MSD=1 cm20=0.5 mm.

LC=MSDn=0.5 mm10=0.05 mm=5×10-2 mm

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment, a set of reading are obtained as follows - $1.24 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.25 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.23 \mathrm{~mm}$, 1.21 mm. The expected least count of the instrument used in recording these readings is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
question: In an experiment, a set of reading are obtained as follows - $1.24 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.25 \mathrm{~mm}, 1.23 \mathrm{~mm}$, 1.21 mm. The expected least count of the instrument used in recording these readings is $\_\_\_\_$ mm.
solution:
Least count is the smallest division readable on an instrument scale.
Given readings are 1.24 mm, 1.25 mm, 1.23 mm, 1.21 mm, all expressed to two decimal places.
The precision of measurement shows differences as small as 0.01 mm (e.g., 1.25 - 1.24 = 0.01 mm).
This indicates all readings can be recorded to the nearest 0.01 mm, which represents the smallest measurable division.
Therefore, the least count of the instrument is 0.01 mm.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment the angles are required to be measured using an instrument. 29 divisions of the main scale exactly coincide with the 30 divisions of the vernier scale. If the smallest division of the main scale is half-a-degree $\left(=0.5^{\circ}\right)$, then the least count of the instrument is
question: In an experiment the angles are required to be measured using an instrument. 29 divisions of the main scale exactly coincide with the 30 divisions of the vernier scale. If the smallest division of the main scale is half-a-degree $\left(=0.5^{\circ}\right)$, then the least count of the instrument is
